coredata.dc:title: Novel Zn/Co–N co-doped carbon quantum dot-based “on-off-on” fluorescent sensor for Fe(III) and ascorbic acid
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2022 The Authors. Published by Elsevier B.V.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Authors. Published by Elsevier B.V.
coredata.dc:creator: Xu, Ouwen
Yang, Jing
Song, Hanyang
Dong, Luzheng
Xia, Ji
Zhu, Xiashi
coredata.dc:description: In this work, the metal (Zn, Co) and non-metal (N) co doped carbon quantum dots (Zn/Co-NCDs) was prepared by hydrothermal method, and characterized by FT-IR, TEM and XPS. It was found that bimetal doped could effectively improve the quantum yield (QY) of carbon quantum dots. The presence of Fe(III) could "turn off" the fluorescence signal, and the addition of ascorbic acid (AA) could "turn on" the fluorescence signal. An "on-off-on" fluorescent probe based on Zn/ Co-NCDs for Fe(III) and AA analysis was established. The liner ranges were 0.03–20.00 μg/mL and 0.69–20.00 μg/mL with the LODs of 0.010 μg/mL and 0.23 μg/mL, respectively. The method was successfully applied to the determination real samples with satisfactory results.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon quantum dots
Metal doped
“on-off-on” fluorescent probe
Fe(III)
Ascorbic acid

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Selectivity and anti-interference ability of Fe (III)/AA detection

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) TEM image of Zn/Co-NCDs (inset: HRTEM of Zn/Co-NCDs); (B) particle size distribution of Zn/Co-NCDs.
FT-IR spectra of Zn/Co-NCDs and diphenylaminourea.
(A) XPS spectra of Zn/Co-NCDs; (B)-(F) High-resolution XPS of Co2p, Zn2p, C1s, N1s and O1s of Zn/Co-NCD.

(A) Fluorescence spectra of Zn/Co-NCDs at different excitation wavelengths; (B) excitation peak, emission peak and UV spectrum of Zn/Co-NCDs; (C) “On-off-on” schematic diagram of Zn/Co-NCDs(the added amount of Fe (III) and AA in the Fig. 4c was 20 mg/mL, respectively).

(A) ΔFoff and ΔFon of Zn/Co-NCDs at different pH, (B) ΔFoff and ΔFon of Zn/Co-NCDs at different reaction time; (C) ΔFoff and ΔFon of Zn/Co-NCDs at different temperature; (D) ΔFoff and ΔFon of Zn/Co-NCDs at different ionic strength.

(A) Linear relationship between Zn/Co-NCDs and Fe (III) concentration (inset: fluorescence spectrum in the presence of 0.00–20.00 mg Fe (III)), (B) linear relationship between Zn/Co-NCDs -Fe (III) and AA concentration (inset: fluorescence spectrum in the presence of 0.00–20.00 mg AA).

(A) The fluorescence intensity ratio of Zn/Co-NCDs before and after the presence of different metal ions, (B) the fluorescence intensity ratio of Zn/Co-NCDs -Fe (III) before and after the presence of different interferents substances.

(A) Fluorescence quenching Stern-Volmer plot of Zn/Co-NCDs; (B) UV–vis absorption spectra of Zn/Co-NCDs, Zn/Co-NCDs +Fe (III), Zn/Co-NCDs -Fe (III)+AA; (C) Zeta potentials of Zn/Co-NCDs, Zn/Co-NCDs +Fe (III), Zn/Co-NCDs-Fe (III) + AA.
Analysis performance in detection of Fe(III) and AA.
Allowable interference multiple of Fe (III) / AA detection.
Detection of AA in food and drug samples.
Detection of Fe (III) in environmental water samples.
Detection of AA in pharmaceutical samples.
Comparison of methods for detecting Fe (III) / AA.

head.ce:title: Novel Zn/Co–N co-doped carbon quantum dot-based “on-off-on” fluorescent sensor for Fe(III) and ascorbic acid

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Guangling, School of Chemistry & Chemical Engineering, Yangzhou University, Yangzhou 225002, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Guangling, School of Chemistry & Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Guangling, School of Chemistry & Chemical Engineering, Yangzhou University, Yangzhou 225002, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this work, the metal (Zn, Co) and non-metal (N) co doped carbon quantum dots (Zn/Co-NCDs) was prepared by hydrothermal method, and characterized by FT-IR, TEM and XPS. It was found that bimetal doped could effectively improve the quantum yield (QY) of carbon quantum dots. The presence of Fe(III) could "turn off" the fluorescence signal, and the addition of ascorbic acid (AA) could "turn on" the fluorescence signal. An "on-off-on" fluorescent probe based on Zn/ Co-NCDs for Fe(III) and AA analysis was established. The liner ranges were 0.03–20.00 μg/mL and 0.69–20.00 μg/mL with the LODs of 0.010 μg/mL and 0.23 μg/mL, respectively. The method was successfully applied to the determination real samples with satisfactory results.

The TEM image is shown that the Zn/Co-NCDs are spherical particles with the particle size of 4–5 nm), and the average particle size is 4.7 nm. HRTEM images display that Zn/Co-NCDs have lattice stripes (0.23 nm), which can be attributed to the (100) in-plane lattice of graphene.
Image, graphical abstract

ce:list-item.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Fe (III) is one of the heavy metal ions that cause many environmental problems, even if the concentration is very low, it will cause a serious impact on the surrounding environment and organisms. On the contrary, Fe (III) is also the basic element of heme and plays an important role in oxygen metabolism [1]. Ascorbic acid (AA) is a natural organic compound and an essential nutrient for the human body, which can prevent diseases caused by free radicals, especially Parkinson's disease and cancer [2]. Therefore, a simple and rapid method for analyzing the above substances is urgently needed for biomedical, chemical, diagnostic, and pathological research.

Carbon quantum dots (CDs) is a fluorescent carbon nano- material with a diameter of less than 10 nm, and it has attracted extensive attention and research since Xu et al. first discovered it in 2004 [3]. CDs has many excellent properties of adjustable fluorescence emission, good water solubility, good biocompatibility, low toxicity, good chemical inertness, environmental friendliness, etc., which was used in chemical sensing [4], biosensor [5], cell imaging [5], medical diagnosis [6], etc. The main synthesis methods include the hydrothermal method [7] and the microwave-assisted method [8]. The hydrothermal method has the advantages of low-cost, simple operation, etc., which is a common method for preparing CDs.

Functionalization of CDs is an effective method to improve the photophysical and photochemical properties, which included element doping, compositing and surface modification [9]. Heteroatomic doping of CDs can regulate the photoelectric properties of CDs and improve the fluorescence quantum yield (QY). Both metallic and nonmetallic elements can be used for the heteroatomic doping of CDs. The doping of non-metallic elements such as N, S, and P will produce n-type and p-type carriers, change the electronic structure and adjust the functional groups on the surface of CDs [10]. In our previous publications, CDs had been functionalized by nitrogen-doped to improve fluorescence properties [11,12]. The introduction of metals can adjust the energy band structure of CDs and prevent the precursor from over-consuming amino and carboxyl groups during dehydration and carbonization through chelation between metal ions and chemical groups [13]. The element of Zn(II) is an important element that assists the electron transfer process and is an essential trace element in the biological system. Zn(II) can exist in Zn-doped CDs (Zn-CDs) in the form of ZnO as a promising metal dopant for CDs, enhancing its charge and energy transfer [14]. Zhang et al. [15] found that Co(II) doped CDs will show the characteristics from surface trap-related emission to Co-related emission and showed the unique intraparticle Förster resonance energy transfer (IPFRET), which may help to increase the interaction between the analyte and surface groups of CDs and improve the sensitivity of CDs fluorescent probes. There seem to be few reports of bimetallic - nonmetallic co-doping CDs

In this paper, bimetal (Zn, Co) and non-metal (N) co-doped carbon quantum dots (Zn/Co-NCDs) were prepared by a hydrothermal method. The doping of bimetal can effectively improve the QY of Zn/Co-NCDs. The presence of Fe(III) could "turn off" the fluorescence signal, and the addition of AA can "turn on" the fluorescence signal. An "on-off-on" fluorescent probe based on Zn/Co-NCDs can be established, and successfully applied to the determination of Fe(III) and AA in real samples.

Diphenylsemicarbazide was purchased from Shanghai Macklin Biochemical Co., Ltd (Shanghai, China). Ascorbic acid (AA) was purchased from Shanghai Energy Chemical Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Cobalt sulfate, zinc chloride, sodium acetate, glacial acetic acid, ammonium chloride and ammonia were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd (Shanghai, China). Fe (III) standard solution was purchased from Beijing North Weiye Measurement Technology Research Institute (Beijing, China).
All reagents were analytical reagent grade, and the water used in the experiment was ultrapure.

Fluorescence measurements were performed on the F-7000 fluorescence spectrophotometer (Hitachi, Japan). The UV–vis absorption spectra were recorded on the Thermo-1780 UV–vis spectrophotometer (TMO, USA). Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) was recorded on TENSOR 27 FTIR spectrometer (Brooke, Germany). The hydrothermal reaction was carried out in DZF-6022 vacuum drying oven (Shanghai Yiheng, China). TEM was recorded on JEM-2100 transmission electron microscope (Japan Electronics, Japan). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was recorded on ESCALAB 250Xi X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (TMO, USA). Zeta potential and particle size distribution were measured by ZEN3690 potential analyzer (Malvern, UK).

In this experiment, Zn/Co-NCDs was synthesized by hydrothermal method. The specific steps were as follows: firstly, 0.3 g diphenylsemicarbazide, 0.1 g cobalt sulfate and 0.1 g zinc chloride were mixed into 30 mL aqueous solution, then put into a polytetrafluoroethylene autoclave and heated at 200 °C for 12 h. After cooling to room temperature, the solution was dialyzed against ultrapure water through a dialysis membrane (MW = 1000 Da) for 24 h while ultrapure water was changed in an interval of 4 h. Finally, the Zn/Co-NCDs solution was obtained and stored at 4 °C for future analysis.

1.0 mL Zn/Co-NCDs solution was mixed with the buffer solution and detection solution to form a 4 mL solution system. The fluorescence spectra of the solution system were recorded under the conditions that the excitation wavelength was set at 315 nm, and emission wavelength was set at 420 nm, and slits of 5.0 nm and 10.0 nm, respectively.

Fluorescence quantum yield was generally measured by the reference method [16]. At the same excitation wavelength, the fluorescence intensity and the area of the fluorescence spectrum of diluted solutions of sample and reference substance were measured respectively. Then the absorbance of UV–vis at the same excitation wavelength was measured. These values were substituted for the formula. The formula was as follow:
φ f
=
Y s
×
F u
F s
×
A s
A u
where φf represents the quantum yield and Ys represents the fluorescence quantum yield of the reference substance. A represents the absorbance at the excitation wavelength respectively. The subscript "s" refers to the reference substance and "u" refers to the sample.

The collected environmental water samples were used after being filtered through a 0.22 μm microfiltration membrane. vitamin C tablet solution was prepared as follows: One vitamin C tablet was dissolved in 50 mL 0.001 mol/L HCl solution. The supernatant was filtered through a 0.22 μm microfiltration membrane. Finally, the samples were collected and used for determination.

The TEM image was shown in Fig. 1 (A). It can be seen that the Zn/Co-NCDs were spherical particles with the particle size of 4–5 nm (Fig. 1(B)), and the average particle size was 4.7 nm. HRTEM images (inset: Fig. 1A) display that Zn/Co-NCDs have lattice stripes (0.23 nm), which can be attributed to the (100) in-plane lattice of graphene.

The FT-IR spectra of Zn/Co-NCDs were shown in Fig. 2 . Comparison of curves between Zn/Co-NCDs (curve 1) and diphenylaminourea(curve 2) displays that the peak of 1490 cm−1–1715 cm−1 which attributed to the benzene ring characteristic complete disappear, proving the diphenylaminourea has carbonized and condensed into carbon nucleus. The Zn/Co-NCDs corresponds to the stretching vibration peak of C-N at 1332 cm−1, indicating that N has successfully doped into Zn/Co-NCDs. Besides, the stretching vibration peak belonging to C-C appeared at 1554 cm−1 and the stretching vibration peak corresponding to C=O at 1637 cm−1, indicating that the surface of Zn/Co-NCDs contains a large amount of -COOH. The phenomenon can be attributed to the protection of Zn/Co-NCDs surface functional groups by metal doping [13]. The wide absorption band from 3104 to 3704 cm−1 was attributed to the stretching vibration of N-H and O-H, indicating that a large number of hydrophilic groups were on the surface of Zn/Co-NCDs including -OH and -NH2.

Zn/Co-NCDs was studied by XPS. Fig. 3 (A) clearly showed that there were five peaks of Co2p, Zn2p, C1s, N1s and O1s in Zn/Co-NCDs. The corresponding contents of 5 elements were 61.21% of C, 19.45% of O, 12.69% of N, 5.05% of Zn and 1.50% of Co respectively. The high-resolution Co 2p spectrum (Fig. 3(B)) can be divided into two peaks of 796.18 eV and 780.76 eV, corresponding to Co2p 3/2 and Co2p 1/2 respectively, which corresponds to cobalt oxide, providing strong evidence that Co exists in the obtained carbon backbone [15]. The high-resolution Zn2p (Fig. 3C) spectrum can be divided into two peaks of 1044.82 eV and 1021.78 eV, corresponding to Zn2p 1/2 and Zn 2p 3/2 respectively, which proves the existence of ZnO and indicating that Zn (II) can exist in Zn/Co-NCD in the form of ZnO [14]. The above results showes that Co and Zn were successfully doped into CDs as metal dopants. In addition, the peaks at 284.18, 285.53 and 287.93 eV in the high-resolution C1s spectrum (Fig. 3(D)) belong to C-C/C=C, C-O/C-N and C=O groups respectively. In the high-resolution N1s spectrum (Fig. 3(E)), the 400.13 and 398.33 eV peaks can be assigned to C-N-C and C-N groups. The two peaks of 531.73 eV and 530.93 eV in the O1s spectrum (Fig. 3(F)) were attributed to C-O and C=O, respectively. The above XPS results further confirmed the successful preparation of Zn/Co-NCDs, with a large number of -OH, -COOH and -NH2 on the surface.

The optical properties of Zn/Co-NCDs were investigated by fluorescence spectra and UV–vis absorption spectra. The fluorescence emission spectra of Zn/Co-NCDs under excitations of 290–470 nm were shown in Fig. 4 (A). The fluorescence intensity of Zn/Co-NCDs increased first and then decreased with the redshift of excitation wavelength, and the fluorescence displayed an excitation-dependent character. The above phenomena benefit from the protection of the surface groups of Zn/Co-NCDs, so that the surface defects were evenly distributed and the fluorescence emission stable [16].

Fig. 4(B) showed that (1) the optimal excitation wavelength of Zn/Co-NCDs at 315 nm, with the optimal emission wavelength of 420 nm (QY = 38.6%); (2) there was a π-π * transition absorption peak corresponding to C=C at 220 nm in the UV–vis absorption spectrum of Zn/Co-NCDs, which was consistent with the fluorescence spectra.

As shown in Fig. 4(C), the fluorescence intensity was efficiently decreased in presence of Fe (III), which indicated that Fe (III) can effectively quench the fluorescence of Zn/Co-NCDs and “turn off” the fluorescence emission signal. Then the intensity of fluorescence can increase after AA adding to the solution, which means AA could restore the fluorescence and “turn on” the signal. Therefore, the Zn/Co-NCDs can be used as an “on-off-on” fluorescent probe for Fe (III) and AA detection.
The pH, time, temperature and ionic strength conditions were optimized by recording the fluorescence intensity difference (△Foff, △Fon) before and after adding Fe (III) and AA.

The influence of pH on △Foff and △Fon are shown in Fig. 5 (A). It can be seen that △Foff and△Fon have the same trend which increases in the range of pH 3.0–7.0 and then decreases in the range of 7.0–11.0. The reason for the phenomenon may be that (1) the amino group and the carboxyl group on the surface of Zn/Co-NCDs tended to increasingly deprotonate in the pH range of 3.0–7.0, leading to the enhancement of the interaction between Zn/Co-NCDs and Fe (III) (ΔFoff↑) [17], (2) the ascorbate anion (AH−, with pKa1 of 4.25) was the predominant species of AA when pH > pKa1, and it has a higher ability of AA to reduce Fe (III) (ΔFon↑)due to it was easily deprotonated to radical cation in the medium of pH>4.25 [18] when the pH range was 7.0–11.0, (3) the side-reaction coefficient of hydroxyl increased, which weakened the interaction between Zn/Co-NCDs and Fe (III) (ΔFoff↓) [19], (4) the interaction between AA and Fe (III) weakened because the enediol group and lactone group of AA was easily oxidized in the alkaline medium (ΔFon↓) [20]. In this experiment, pH 7.0 was selected as the optimal pH for detection.
The effect of reaction time on △Foff and△Fon was shown in Fig. 5(B) that the△Foff and△Fon tend to be stable after a while. The results showed that the best quenching time and recovery time are10 min.
As shown in Fig. 5(C), △Foff decreases with increasing temperature and △Fon increases with temperature. Finally, the room temperature (20 °C) was selected for the experiment.
From the experiment, the ionic strength does not affect the determination process (Fig. 5(D)).

As shown in Fig. 6 (A), the fluorescence intensity of Zn/Co-NCDs decreased with the increase of Fe (III) concentration of 0.00–20.00 μg/mL added. When the 0.00–20.00 μg/mL concentration of AA was added in Zn/Co-NCDs-Fe (III) system, the fluorescence gradually increased (Fig. 6(B)). Therefore, the analytical performance of Zn/Co-NCDs as a fluorescent probe for detecting Fe (III) and AA could be obtained, the analysis performance parameters were listed in Table 1 .

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selectivity and anti-interference ability of Fe (III)/AA detection
ce:section.ce:para: As shown in Fig. 7 (A) and (B), only Fe (III) showed a significant fluorescence quenching effect to Zn/Co-NCDs when metal ions and interferents of the same concentration were added to the system. After the potential interference of the same concentration was added, AA was an obvious fluorescence restore to Zn/Co-NCDs-Fe(III). The above result indicated that Zn/Co-NCDs has a high selectivity for detecting Fe (III) and AA. The allowable interference multiple (±5%) was determined by adding higher concentrations of interfering metal ions and potential interferences during the detection of Fe (III) and AA, respectively. The results were shown in Table 2 , the fluorescence intensity will not be significantly affected even if there were high concentrations of interfering potential in the system. The results above proved that there were a satisfactory selectivity and a good anti-interference ability of Zn/Co-NCDs for the detection of Fe (III) and AA.

To investigate the applicability of Zn/Co-NCDs in food samples, the concentration of AA in vitamin C tablets was determined. As shown in Table 5 , the content of vitamin C tablets was 97.66 mg/g with the recovery of 95.17–104.88%. The above results prove that Zn/Co-NCDs has the potential to detect AA in pharmaceutical.

As shown in Table 6 , Zn/Co-NCDs have different application performances when used as fluorescent probes due to their properties have changed compared with NCDs without Zn/Co doping. Zn/Co NCDs exhibit a wider linear range and lower LODs compared with the undoped NCDs, which benefits from the improvement of QY after Zn/Co doping. The red shift of the excitation wavelength and emission wavelength could also help to reduce the interference caused by environmental factors [21].

The method for determining Fe (III) and AA was compared with other methods. It can be seen from Table 7 that the Zn/Co-NCDs has lower LODs and wide linear ranges, indicating that Zn/Co-NCDs can be used as an effective “on-off-on” fluorescent probe for the determination of Fe(III) and AA.

To clarify the quenching mechanism, the Stern-Volmer parameters were investigated at 20 °C, 40 °C, and 60 °C. As seen from Fig. 8 (A), the values of KSV decreased with the increase of temperature, which suggested that the quenching was the static quenching mechanism [22].

Static quenching was caused by the formation of a nonfluorescent ground state complex between CDs and the quencher [23], and the quencher would change the UV–vis spectra of CDs [24] Therefore, the formation of the nonfluorescent ground state complex in this quenching process can be confirmed by UV spectral data. As shown in Fig. 8(B), the curve of Zn/Co-NCDs +Fe (III) appeared with a new absorption peak at the wavelength of 320 nm, explaining there was a formation of ground state complexes between Zn/Co-NCDs and Fe (III).

The zeta potential of the quenching process in each stage was determined. As shown in Fig. 8(C), the zeta potential of Zn/Co-NCDs was measured to be −21.9 mV due to several exposed -COOH on the surface. Because the -COOH was coordinated with Fe (III) to form the ground state complex, the zeta potential of Zn/Co-NCDs increased to −5.1 mV after adding Fe (III), This result was consistent with the UV–vis spectra. Therefore, the formation of a non-fluorescent ground state complex between Zn/Co-NCDs and Fe (III) was the reason for the fluorescence “off” (Fig. 9 ).

The restoration of fluorescence in the system was usually attributed to the competitive coordination mechanism [25]. As shown in Fig. 8(C), the zeta potential of Zn/Co-NCDs-Fe(III) decreased to −19.2 mV after adding AA due to the high reducibility of AA to Fe (III). It was consistent with the decrease of absorption peak in the UV–vis spectrum, suggesting that there was a redox reaction between Fe(III) and AA, and led to the reduction of Fe (III) to Fe (II). The chelating ability of Fe(II) to Zn/Co-NCDs was lower than Fe (III), resulting in fluorescence “on” (Fig. 9).

In this paper, a bimetal and non-metal co-doped carbon quantum dots (Zn/Co-NCDs) was prepared. Nitrogen doping can dope into the carbon core and create nitrogen functional groups on the surface, and metal doping can protect the surface functional groups of CDs, thus effectively improving the fluorescence intensity and the sensitivity of Zn/Co-NCDs as a fluorescence probe. The fluorescence of Zn/Co-NCDs could be “turned on/off” by Fe (III)/AA, and the "on-off-on" process can establish the selective detection probe for Fe (III) and AA. Finally, the method was successfully applied to the analysis of real samples with satisfactory results.

This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (21375117) and a project funded by the Priority Academic Program Development of Jiangsu Higher Education Institutions and Postgraduate research/practice innovation program of Jiangsu province.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare no competing interests.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Facile synthesis of nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots with chitosan for fluorescent detection of Fe3+
Polymers

Anal. Chim. Acta

Acta. 431 (2001) 239–247. https://doi.org/10.1016/S0003-2670(00)01330-1.

Talanta

ACS Appl. Mater. Interfaces

Lin, Drug-derived bright and color-tunable N-doped carbon dots for cell imaging and sensitive detection of Fe3+ in living cells, ACS Appl. Mater. Interfaces. 9 (2017) 7399–7405. https://doi.org/10.1021/acsami.6b13954.

Trends Biotechnol.

Spectrosc.. 206 (2019) 65–71. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.saa.2018.07.082.

B-Biol.. 161 (2016) 154–161. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jphotobiol.2016.05.017.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Simple and selective determination of 6-thioguanine by using polyethylenimine (PEI) functionalized carbon dots
Talanta

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: H. Zeng, L. Li, Y. Ding, Q. Zhuang, Simple and selective determination of 6-thioguanine by using polyethylenimine (PEI) functionalized carbon dots, Talanta. 178 (2018) 879–885. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.talanta.2017.09.087.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Carbon quantum dots: synthesis, properties and applications
